title: Non-volatile superconducting tunnelling magnetoresistance memory enabled by exchange-field gap engineering
authors: Sonam Bhakat, Pushpak Banerjee, Ahmedullah Aziz, Jackson Miller, Avradeep Pal
abstract: Scalable, low-dissipation memory operating below 4 K is a critical requirement for superconducting and quantum computing systems. Existing cryogenic memory technologies rely on CMOS derivatives or hybrid architectures that incur leakage, refresh overhead or limited compatibility with superconducting logic. Here we demonstrate a superconducting tunnelling magnetoresistance device that functions as a non-volatile cryogenic memory element across the full superconducting temperature range. By integrating a de Gennes spin valve with a superconducting tunnel junction in a current perpendicular-to-plane geometry, we realise exchange-field control of the superconducting energy gap. This produces two magnetically switchable gap voltages and robust quasiparticle tunnelling magnetoresistance down to 0.25 K.The device operates at millivolt bias with nanowatt-level read power and zero standby dissipation. Its vertical junction architecture and Nb-based materials platform enable compatibility with superconducting logic and scalable cryogenic memory arrays.
summary: This paper presents a new type of superconducting memory element that uses magnetic fields to tune the superconducting energy gap. Unlike previous technologies that only work in a narrow temperature range, this device remains functional and non-volatile across the entire superconducting temperature range. It operates with extremely low power consumption (nanowatts) and is compatible with existing niobium-based superconducting logic. This makes it a promising candidate for building scalable, energy-efficient hardware for quantum and neuromorphic computing.
main_problem: The lack of scalable, energy-efficient, and non-volatile memory technologies compatible with superconducting and quantum computing systems operating below 4K.
main_result: Demonstration of a superconducting tunneling magnetoresistance device that achieves non-volatile, temperature-independent switching of the superconducting energy gap via exchange-field engineering.
method: The researchers used ultra-high vacuum sputtering to grow a multi-layered thin film stack and employed multi-step lithography to create mesa-type junctions, followed by tunneling spectroscopy and magneto-transport measurements.
model_or_system: A vertical current-perpendicular-to-plane (CPP) device architecture consisting of a de Gennes spin valve integrated with a superconducting tunnel junction, specifically a GdN/Nb/GdN/Al-AlOx/Nb multilayer stack.
key_observables: Superconducting energy gap (delta), Quasiparticle Tunneling Magnetoresistance (QTMR), differential conductance (dI/dV), and transition temperature (Tc).
important_parameters: Operating temperatures down to 0.25K, nanowatt-level read power, zero standby dissipation, and 95% spin polarization.
main_assumptions: The device is modeled as two parallel segments where the S1 electrode is subject to Zeeman splitting and spin-orbit scattering, and the S2 electrode acts as a stable, magnetically decoupled reference.
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows CIP resistance vs. magnetic field and temperature; Figure 4c shows bias-dependent QTMR.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the bottleneck in cryogenic hardware, describes the device architecture and fabrication, presents magneto-transport and tunneling spectroscopy results, validates the mechanism through numerical modeling of the density of states, and discusses applications in neuromorphic and cryogenic computing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
